claims.claim_text: A defense contractor stored classified automation scripts on a developer's personal GitHub account for three years, causing a $2.3 million security incident.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The security officer's face went pale as she reviewed the audit findings. A defense contractor's classified automation scripts had been stored on a developer's personal GitHub account for three years. No malicious intent—the engineer just needed version control and GitHub was what he knew. The resulting security incident cost the company $2.3 million in investigation costs, remediation, and lost contract opportunities. The fix? A Git server that could run inside their air-gapped network.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Audit findings showed that sensitive automation scripts had been stored outside the organization's approved environment. That kind of setup creates serious security and compliance risk, and it is one reason teams adopt an internal Git server for controlled networks.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with named context and fake-precise cost figures, so the safe fix is to keep the lesson and remove the fabricated specifics.

claims.claim_text: A semiconductor fab on Raspberry Pi 4 hardware found GitLab Omnibus needed 8 GB RAM and GitHub Enterprise Server would cost $180,000 annually.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Three thousand miles away, a semiconductor fab faced a different problem. Their equipment automation team needed version control, but their entire edge compute infrastructure ran on Raspberry Pi 4s with 4GB RAM each. The IT team tried GitLab Omnibus—it demanded 8GB RAM minimum just to start. The licensing quote for GitHub Enterprise Server came back at $180,000 annually. For a Git server. To store shell scripts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Other teams face a different constraint: they need version control on very small hardware and cannot justify a heavyweight platform or enterprise licensing just to store internal scripts.
claims.rationale: The paragraph mixes an unattributed incident with exact hardware and pricing claims that are not honestly supportable from an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Gitea is a self-hosted Git service written in Go, forked from Gogs in November 2016, and distributed as a standalone `gitea` binary.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 4
claims.rationale: The upstream README directly states that Gitea is written in Go, was forked from Gogs in November 2016, and builds to a `gitea` binary.

claims.claim_text: Forgejo is a community fork of Gitea with different governance under Codeberg.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 5
claims.rationale: Forgejo's own announcement is the right primary source for the governance split, but `forgejo.org` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Gitea and Forgejo are 95% identical today.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 5
claims.anchor_text: They're 95% identical today, but the philosophical differences matter if you're choosing for the long term.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The projects remained closely related after the fork, but governance and roadmap differences still matter if you're choosing for the long term.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage is not traceable to a stable primary source, so the number should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Gitea runs in ~100 MB RAM, GitLab requires 4 GB minimum, and one hobbyist runs 15 Gitea instances on a 4 GB VPS for €5/month.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: **Gitea runs in ~100MB of RAM** - GitLab requires 4GB minimum. A hobbyist in Germany runs 15 separate Gitea instances on a single 4GB VPS—one per open source project—spending less on hosting than a single GitLab license would cost. The entire setup costs him €5/month.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Gitea is much lighter than a full GitLab deployment** - That makes it attractive for hobby, lab, and small-team self-hosting on modest infrastructure.
claims.rationale: This bullet combines unsupported exact resource numbers with an unattributed anecdote and a dated price.

claims.claim_text: Forgejo emerged in late 2022 after a dispute over Gitea's move to for-profit governance, under Codeberg e.V.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 2
claims.rationale: The underlying governance event appears real and the Forgejo announcement discusses it directly, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Codeberg's Forgejo instance has over 100,000 users and 150,000+ repositories.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 3
claims.anchor_text: **Codeberg.org runs entirely on Forgejo** - Over 100,000 users and 150,000+ repositories run on Codeberg's Forgejo instance, proving the platform scales far beyond "toy project" status. It's become the de facto home for developers who want a GitHub alternative without corporate ownership.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Codeberg.org runs on Forgejo** - That makes it a useful real-world example that Forgejo can power a substantial public forge.
claims.rationale: The exact counts are volatile and I did not find an allowlisted live source that honestly backs those specific numbers.

claims.claim_text: Gogs was created in 2014 as a self-hosted GitHub clone.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 4, sentence 1
claims.anchor_text: Gogs (Go Git Service) was created in 2014 as a "self-hosted GitHub clone."
claims.suggested_rewrite: Gogs was an earlier self-hosted Git service from which Gitea later forked.
claims.rationale: The exact year-and-framing sentence is not backed by an allowlisted primary source in hand, so the safe edit is to keep the lineage without the precision.

claims.claim_text: Gitea was forked from Gogs in November 2016.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 4, sentence 2
claims.rationale: The upstream README explicitly says Gitea has been forked from Gogs since November 2016.

claims.claim_text: Forgejo forked from Gitea in 2022 after the Gitea commercialization dispute.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 4, sentence 3
claims.rationale: The Forgejo launch post is the right primary source for this claim, but it is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Gitea supports SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MSSQL databases, plus configurable LFS/cache/search backends.
claims.span_hint: section: Supported Backends table
claims.rationale: Gitea's configuration cheat sheet directly lists these backends, but `docs.gitea.com` is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Gitea's `OFFLINE_MODE` disables CDN use for static files and Gravatar fetching.
claims.span_hint: section: Configuration Deep Dive, app.ini example and later air-gap guidance
claims.rationale: The configuration cheat sheet describes `OFFLINE_MODE` directly, but the authoritative page is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: `INSTALL_LOCK` prevents access to Gitea's installation page after setup.
claims.span_hint: section: Configuration Deep Dive and Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The configuration cheat sheet states what `INSTALL_LOCK` does, but that source is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Gitea supports LDAP authentication sources, including BindDN and simple-auth LDAP flows.
claims.span_hint: section: LDAP/Active Directory Integration
claims.rationale: The Gitea authentication docs cover LDAP support directly, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Gitea can act as an OAuth2/OpenID Connect provider.
claims.span_hint: section: OAuth2/OIDC (Keycloak Example)
claims.rationale: Gitea's OAuth2 provider documentation directly supports this capability, but it is hosted on an off-allowlist domain.

claims.claim_text: Starting with Gitea 1.19, Gitea Actions are built in and are designed to be mostly compatible with GitHub Actions.
claims.span_hint: section: Gitea Actions: GitHub-Compatible CI/CD
claims.rationale: The Gitea Actions overview states both the 1.19 introduction and the compatibility framing, but the primary doc is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: `[actions].DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL` accepts `github` or `self` and controls where unqualified actions are fetched from.
claims.span_hint: section: Setting Up Actions and Actions Compatibility Notes
claims.rationale: The comparison docs explicitly describe the `github` and `self` behavior, but the source is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Gitea supports pull and push repository mirroring for branches, tags, and commits.
claims.span_hint: section: Migration Strategies, mirroring examples
claims.rationale: The repo mirror documentation directly supports this feature claim, but the domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Gitea can migrate repositories from GitHub/GitLab/Gogs and import items such as issues and pull requests.
claims.span_hint: section: Migration Strategies, GitHub to Gitea
claims.rationale: The migration docs describe built-in migration from GitHub and mention migrating issues and pull requests, but the doc host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A semiconductor fab in Arizona had 2,400 equipment scripts in shared folders without version control or rollback.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, The Situation, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The fab's automation team maintained 2,400 equipment scripts controlling everything from wafer handling to chemical vapor deposition. For years, these scripts lived in shared network folders with names like `etch_recipe_FINAL_v3_REALLY_FINAL.sh`. No version control. No audit trail. No rollback capability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The automation team maintained many equipment-control scripts in shared folders with inconsistent naming. Without version control, they also lacked a clean audit trail and reliable rollback path.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed composite incident with fake-precise operational detail.

claims.claim_text: The FDA demanded traceability for every script change at a semiconductor fab, putting a $340 million expansion approval at risk.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, The Situation, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: The FDA (semiconductor fabs fall under pharmaceutical-grade regulations) demanded complete traceability for every script modification. Who changed what, when, and why? The answer was "we don't know"—and that answer was about to cost them their expansion approval.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A compliance audit demanded traceability for script changes, and the team could not answer basic questions about who changed what and when.
claims.rationale: The regulatory framing is dubious and the whole paragraph reads as a fabricated audit anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The factory-floor case required complete air-gapping for ITAR compliance.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Requirements bullet 1
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Operate in a fully isolated network
claims.rationale: The named regulatory basis is not sourced and should be collapsed to the operational requirement.

claims.claim_text: The factory-floor case had to run on Raspberry Pi 4 hardware with 4 GB RAM each.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Requirements bullet 2
claims.anchor_text: - Run on existing Raspberry Pi 4 infrastructure (4GB RAM each)
claims.rationale: The exact hardware spec is part of an unattributed story and does not need to survive the rewrite.

claims.claim_text: The factory-floor case had to be deployed in 6 weeks before a re-audit.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Requirements bullet 6
claims.anchor_text: - Deployed in 6 weeks before the re-audit
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Be deployable on a short compliance deadline
claims.rationale: This is fake-precise timeline detail inside an unattributed anecdote.

claims.claim_text: GitLab quoted $127,000 annually plus $45,000 in professional services for the air-gapped deployment.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Why Not GitLab?, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The IT team's first call was to GitLab sales. The quote came back: $127,000 annually for a self-managed license, plus $45,000 in professional services for the air-gapped deployment.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The team ruled out a heavier enterprise platform because licensing and deployment overhead did not fit the budget or the timeline.
claims.rationale: These are unattributed, dated price quotes embedded in a likely composite incident.

claims.claim_text: GitHub Enterprise Server would have cost $21 per user per month with a 500-user minimum.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Why Not GitLab?, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: GitHub Enterprise Server? $21 per user per month, minimum 500 users. That's $126,000/year for a Git server to store shell scripts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Per-seat enterprise Git hosting can be hard to justify when the immediate need is basic internal version control for scripts and automation.
claims.rationale: This is another unattributed pricing snapshot that is not honestly supportable as written.

claims.claim_text: The factory-floor deployment preserved a $340 million expansion and avoided a 6+ month delay.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Results, Total first-year value paragraph
claims.anchor_text: **Total first-year value**: The $340 million expansion proceeded on schedule. The alternatives—either paying for enterprise Git licensing or failing the re-audit—would have delayed production by 6+ months.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Outcome**: The deployment reduced audit risk and avoided buying a heavier platform for a narrow internal use case.
claims.rationale: This is fabricated financial-impact storytelling with false precision.

claims.claim_text: SQLite handled 50 users with no performance issues and zero maintenance in the factory-floor case.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Lessons Learned, item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. **SQLite is fine for small teams** - 50 users, no performance issues, zero maintenance
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **SQLite can be workable for small deployments** - validate concurrency, backup, and maintenance requirements against your own workload.
claims.rationale: The exact user-count and performance verdict are anecdotal and unsupported.

claims.claim_text: On Raspberry Pi, CPU stayed around 10% and RAM around 98 MB in the factory-floor case.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Lessons Learned, item 4
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Raspberry Pi handles it** - CPU barely touched 10%, RAM comfortable at 98MB
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Small hardware can be enough** - lightweight Git hosting is often viable on modest systems when the workload is limited.
claims.rationale: These exact utilization numbers are not supportable and should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Every commit in the factory-floor case was signed and every change traceable.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story, Lessons Learned, item 5
claims.anchor_text: 5. **The audit trail saved them** - Every commit signed, every change traceable, every rollback documented
claims.suggested_rewrite: 5. **Version control improves traceability** - it gives teams a clearer record of changes, authorship, and rollback history.
claims.rationale: This is still part of the fabricated incident and uses absolute wording that is not sourced.

claims.claim_text: Gitea proves a useful Git server does not need 4 GB of RAM.
claims.span_hint: closing italicized quote
claims.anchor_text: *"Sometimes the best tool is the simplest one that solves your problem. Gitea proves that a Git server doesn't need 4GB of RAM to be useful."*
claims.suggested_rewrite: *"Sometimes the best tool is the simplest one that solves your problem. Gitea illustrates the appeal of a lightweight self-hosted Git service."*
claims.rationale: The closing line repeats an unsupported exact RAM figure and should be generalized.

further_reading.title: go-gitea/gitea
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream repository for Gitea's feature scope, packaging model, and project lineage.

further_reading.title: Gitea Releases
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for verifying the standalone binary release model and current artifact packaging.

further_reading.title: Forgejo
further_reading.why_relevant: Helpful secondary background on the Gitea-to-Forgejo split when readers want context beyond the module.

notes: This module contains two long unattributed anecdotes ('Why This Module Matters' and 'War Story') with many fake-precise numbers, prices, and timelines. Those sections need editorial collapse, not citation sprinkling. Honest primary support for many configuration and feature claims exists on `docs.gitea.com` and `forgejo.org`, but both domains are currently off-allowlist.
